# HTTP library


The HTTP library is a standalone package shared across the iii SDKs. It holds the HTTP primitives
that several SDKs need to agree on: the authentication config, the external invocation config, the
HTTP method type, the buffered request and response types, and the `http` handler helper.

## Packages

| Language | Package | Import name |
| -------- | ------- | ----------- |
| Node | `@iii-dev/http` (npm) | `@iii-dev/http` |
| Python | `iii-http` (PyPI) | `iii_http` |
| Rust | `iii-http` (crate) | `iii_http` |

The root SDK packages (`iii-sdk` on npm, `iii-sdk` on PyPI, `iii-sdk` on crates.io) depend on the
HTTP library and re-export its symbols from their old root paths as deprecated shims. New code should
import from the HTTP library directly.

## HttpAuthConfig

`HttpAuthConfig` is the authentication configuration for HTTP-invoked functions. It is a tagged union
with three members: `hmac` (HMAC signature verification using a shared secret), `bearer` (bearer
token), and `api_key` (API key sent via a custom header). The variant fields match the engine
`auth.rs` definition: `hmac` carries `secret_key`, `bearer` carries `token_key`, and `api_key`
carries `header` and `value_key`.

### Node

```ts
import type { HttpAuthConfig } from '@iii-dev/http'

const auth: HttpAuthConfig = { type: 'bearer', token_key: 'MY_TOKEN' }
```

`HttpAuthConfig` is a union of `{ type: 'hmac'; secret_key }`, `{ type: 'bearer'; token_key }`, and
`{ type: 'api_key'; header; value_key }`.

### Python

```python
from iii_http import HttpAuthBearer, HttpAuthConfig

auth: HttpAuthConfig = HttpAuthBearer(token_key="MY_TOKEN")
```

`HttpAuthConfig` is a union of three Pydantic models: `HttpAuthHmac`, `HttpAuthBearer`, and
`HttpAuthApiKey`. All three models are exported from `iii_http`.

### Rust

```rust
use iii_http::HttpAuthConfig;

let auth = HttpAuthConfig::Bearer { token_key: "MY_TOKEN".to_string() };
```

`HttpAuthConfig` is a tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type", rename_all = "lowercase")]`) with the
`Hmac { secret_key }`, `Bearer { token_key }`, and `ApiKey { header, value_key }` variants. The
`ApiKey` variant serializes its tag as `api_key`.

## HttpInvocationConfig

`HttpInvocationConfig` is the configuration for registering an HTTP-invoked function (Lambda,
Cloudflare Workers, and similar) instead of a local handler. The wire fields are `url`, `method`
(defaulting to `POST`), `timeout_ms`, `headers`, and `auth`.

### Node

```ts
import type { HttpInvocationConfig } from '@iii-dev/http'

const config: HttpInvocationConfig = { url: 'https://example.com/fn', method: 'POST' }
```

### Python

```python
from iii_http import HttpInvocationConfig

config = HttpInvocationConfig(url="https://example.com/fn", method="POST")
```

### Rust

```rust
use iii_http::{HttpInvocationConfig, HttpMethod};

let config = HttpInvocationConfig {
    url: "https://example.com/fn".to_string(),
    method: HttpMethod::Post,
    timeout_ms: None,
    headers: Default::default(),
    auth: None,
};
```

## HttpMethod

`HttpMethod` is the HTTP method for an HTTP-invoked function: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, or
`DELETE`.

In Node and Python, `HttpMethod` is a named alias added alongside the existing inline method literal
on `HttpInvocationConfig`. The `HttpInvocationConfig.method` field keeps its inline literal type, so
the named alias is additive. In Rust, `HttpMethod` is the enum that `HttpInvocationConfig.method`
references directly.

### Node

```ts
import type { HttpMethod } from '@iii-dev/http'

const method: HttpMethod = 'POST'
```

### Python

```python
from iii_http import HttpMethod

method: HttpMethod = "POST"
```

### Rust

```rust
use iii_http::HttpMethod;

let method = HttpMethod::Post;
```

<Info>
The core SDK also defines a second, distinct `HttpMethod` for the HTTP trigger config
(in the Rust core `builtin_triggers` module). That trigger-side method type adds `Head` and
`Options` variants and is unrelated to the `HttpMethod` defined in this library. It stays in the
core SDK and is not part of the HTTP library.
</Info>

## HttpRequest and HttpResponse

`HttpRequest` and `HttpResponse` are the buffered HTTP request and response types. `HttpRequest`
carries the request parameters (path params, query params, body, headers, method, and the raw
request body reader). `HttpResponse` carries `status_code`, `headers`, and `body`.

These names were reclaimed for the buffered types. Before this change the names `HttpRequest` and
`HttpResponse` were deprecated aliases of the streaming `StreamRequest` and `StreamResponse` types.
Those streaming aliases are removed. `HttpRequest` and `HttpResponse` now resolve to the buffered
types in the HTTP library, `StreamRequest` and `StreamResponse` remain the streaming types in the
core SDK, and `ApiRequest` and `ApiResponse` are the new deprecated aliases of the buffered types.

### Node

```ts
import type { HttpRequest, HttpResponse } from '@iii-dev/http'

const response: HttpResponse = { status_code: 200, body: { message: 'ok' } }
```

### Python

```python
from iii_http import HttpRequest, HttpResponse

response = HttpResponse(status_code=200, body={"message": "ok"})
```

`HttpResponse` is a Pydantic model whose `status_code` field also accepts the `statusCode` alias.

### Rust

```rust
use iii_http::HttpResponse;

let response = HttpResponse {
    status_code: 200,
    headers: Default::default(),
    body: serde_json::json!({ "message": "ok" }),
};
```

In Rust, the core SDK `StreamRequest` and `StreamResponse` are type aliases of `iii_http::HttpRequest`
and `iii_http::HttpResponse`.

## http helper

The `http` helper wraps a streaming handler so it receives a typed request and response pair. It
takes a callback `(req, res)` and returns a function the engine can invoke directly. The callback
returns `HttpResponse` or nothing.

### Node

```ts
import { http } from '@iii-dev/http'

iii.registerFunction(
  'my-api',
  http(async (req, res) => {
    res.status(200)
    res.headers({ 'content-type': 'application/json' })
    res.stream.end(JSON.stringify({ hello: 'world' }))
    res.close()
  }),
)
```

### Python

```python
from iii_http import http


@http
async def handler(req, res):
    await res.status(200)
    await res.headers({"content-type": "application/json"})
    await res.writer.write(b'{"hello": "world"}')
    await res.writer.close_async()
```

## D7 type identity

The buffered types and the streaming types are distinct in Node and Python, and aliased in Rust. The
table below describes the end state per language.

| Name | Node | Python | Rust |
| ---- | ---- | ------ | ---- |
| `HttpRequest` / `HttpResponse` | Buffered types in `@iii-dev/http` | Buffered Pydantic models in `iii_http` | `iii_http` structs; core `StreamRequest` / `StreamResponse` alias them |
| `StreamRequest` / `StreamResponse` | Streaming types in core SDK | Streaming types in core SDK | Aliases of `iii_http::HttpRequest` / `HttpResponse` |
| `ApiRequest` / `ApiResponse` | Deprecated aliases of `HttpRequest` / `HttpResponse` | Deprecated aliases of `HttpRequest` / `HttpResponse` | Deprecated re-exports of `iii_http::HttpRequest` / `HttpResponse` |

## Deprecated root re-exports

For backward compatibility, the root SDK still resolves these symbols at their old paths. These
paths are deprecated and emit a deprecation notice. Prefer the HTTP library import shown above.

| Language | Deprecated path | Replacement |
| -------- | --------------- | ----------- |
| Node | `HttpAuthConfig`, `HttpInvocationConfig`, `HttpMethod`, `http` from `iii-sdk` root | the same from `@iii-dev/http` |
| Node | `ApiRequest` / `ApiResponse` from `iii-sdk` root | `HttpRequest` / `HttpResponse` from `@iii-dev/http` |
| Python | `from iii import HttpAuthConfig, HttpInvocationConfig, HttpMethod, http` | the same from `iii_http` |
| Python | `from iii import ApiRequest, ApiResponse` | `from iii_http import HttpRequest, HttpResponse` |
| Rust | `iii_sdk::HttpAuthConfig`, `iii_sdk::HttpInvocationConfig`, `iii_sdk::HttpMethod` | the same from `iii_http` |
| Rust | `iii_sdk::ApiRequest` / `iii_sdk::ApiResponse` | `iii_http::HttpRequest` / `iii_http::HttpResponse` |
